symbols.name: \(V\)
symbols.def: Observed vertices.

symbols.name: \(S\)
symbols.def: Selector vertex.

symbols.name: \(V^\circ\)
symbols.def: \(V^\circ=V\setminus\{S\}\).

symbols.name: \(\mathcal L\)
symbols.def: Atomic selector labels.

symbols.name: \(\ell'\)
symbols.def: A second atomic selector label used in label-coherence conditions.

symbols.name: \(W_\ell\)
symbols.def: Child vertex seriously assigned by label \(\ell\).

symbols.name: \(W_{\ell'}\)
symbols.def: Child vertex seriously assigned by the second label \(\ell'\).

symbols.name: \(z_\ell\)
symbols.def: Value imposed by label \(\ell\).

symbols.name: \(z_{\ell'}\)
symbols.def: Value imposed by the second label \(\ell'\).

symbols.name: \(\mathcal S\)
symbols.def: \(\mathcal S=\{s\subseteq\mathcal L:\ell,\ell'\in s,\ W_\ell=W_{\ell'}\Rightarrow z_\ell=z_{\ell'}\}\).

symbols.name: \(\emptyset\)
symbols.def: The target selector state with no serious assignment.

symbols.name: \(\pi\)
symbols.def: Law of the selector \(S\).

symbols.name: \(\mathcal U\)
symbols.def: \(\mathcal U=\operatorname{supp}(\pi)\).

symbols.name: \(u\)
symbols.def: One supplied selector state.

symbols.name: \(J(u)\)
symbols.def: \(J(u)=\{W_\ell:\ell\in u\}\).

symbols.name: \(z_v(u)\)
symbols.def: \(z_v(u)=z_\ell\) for any \(\ell\in u\) with \(W_\ell=v\).

symbols.name: \(E^{\to}\)
symbols.def: Directed edge copies on \(V\), including edges incident to \(S\); each copy may carry a finite child-label set.

symbols.name: \(E^{\leftrightarrow}\)
symbols.def: Bidirected edge copies on \(V\), including selector-incident copies; each copy may carry a finite child-label set.

symbols.name: \(\bar G\)
symbols.type: labelled selection acyclic directed mixed multigraph
symbols.def: \(\bar G\) has vertex set \(V\), directed and bidirected edge copies \(E^{\to}\) and \(E^{\leftrightarrow}\), and child-label sets on edge copies; \(\bar G^{[s]}\) deletes every edge copy whose label names a vertex in \(J(s)\).

symbols.name: \(A\)
symbols.def: Treatment vertices fixed in the target intervention.

symbols.name: \(a\)
symbols.def: Target treatment value.

symbols.name: \(Y\)
symbols.def: Outcome vertices in the target law.

symbols.name: \(d\)
symbols.def: Treatment vertex used as the parity gate.

symbols.name: \(y\)
symbols.def: Outcome vertex at which the two arms meet.

symbols.name: \(b_L,b_R\)
symbols.def: Initial vertices of the left and right parity arms.

symbols.name: \(P_L,P_R\)
symbols.def: Directed paths from \(b_L\) and \(b_R\), respectively, to \(y\).

symbols.name: \(T_L,T_R\)
symbols.def: \(T_j=\operatorname{v}(P_j)\setminus\{y\}\) for \(j\in\{L,R\}\).

symbols.name: \(e_\star\)
symbols.def: The distinguished bidirected edge copy whose edge-local exogenous law is perturbed; in the two-arm parity construction its endpoints are \(b_L\) and \(b_R\).

symbols.name: \(\mathfrak H\)
symbols.def: \(\mathfrak H=(e_\star,\Omega_\star,\lambda_0,h,\mathbf f,\rho)\) specifies one graph-compatible finite canonical CS-SCM response skeleton and one observed-null but target-nonnull perturbation of an edge-local exogenous law.

symbols.name: \(\mathcal H_{\mathrm{CS}}\)
symbols.def: The set of context-specific response-null certificate tuples for \(\bar G,A,a,Y,\mathcal U\).

symbols.name: \(B_L,B_R\)
symbols.def: Two coordinates of the edge-local exogenous variable \(U_\star\).

symbols.name: \(U_\star\)
symbols.def: The exogenous source associated with \(e_\star\), parent only of the endpoints of that edge copy; in the parity specialization \(U_\star=(B_L,B_R)\).

symbols.name: \(\kappa\)
symbols.def: \(\kappa=\mathbb E[(-1)^{B_L+B_R}]\).

symbols.name: \(X_v\)
symbols.type: binary random variable indexed by \(v\in V^\circ\)
symbols.def: Observed response at nonselector vertex \(v\).

symbols.name: \(M_\kappa\)
symbols.def: The explicit selector-aware parity CS-SCM indexed by \(\kappa\).

symbols.name: \(P_{u,\kappa}\)
symbols.def: \(P_{u,\kappa}=p_{M_\kappa}(X_{V^\circ}\mid S=u)\).

symbols.name: \(Q_\kappa\)
symbols.def: \(Q_\kappa(x_Y)=p_{M_\kappa}(X_Y(a,S=\emptyset)=x_Y)\).

symbols.name: \(\mathcal I\)
symbols.def: A finite LS-ADMMG \(\bar G\), the full observed joint law \(p(X_{V^\circ},S)\), a treatment value \(a\), and an outcome query \(Y\) supplied to \(\mathsf{SS\text{-}ID}\).

symbols.name: \(\mathsf{SS\text{-}ID}\)
symbols.def: The systematic-selection identification algorithm of Lee, Ghassami, and Shpitser.

symbols.name: \(\mathcal C_{\mathrm{cs2}}\)
symbols.def: Finite inputs whose Algorithm 2 candidate loop is exhausted and whose execution reaches the final bare failure return at line 9.

novel.justification: Binary encouragements, treatments, and outcomes are common in the data-fusion settings motivating CS-SCMs. This is an explicitly delimited countermodel domain, not a claim about the general CS-SCM semantics.

assumptions.condition: The directed part of \(\bar G\) on \(V\), including selector-incident edges, is acyclic.
For every \(s\in\mathcal S\) and every \(\ell,\ell'\in s\), \(W_\ell=W_{\ell'}\Rightarrow z_\ell=z_{\ell'}\).

novel.justification: This is the coherent-subset encoding of the child-specific intervention-indicator and intervention-value selector in a binary CS-SCM. It permits different contexts to assign different values to the same child, while forbidding contradictory assignments within one context.

definitions.name: Serious selector assignment
definitions.construction: For every selector state \(s\in\mathcal S\), define \(J(s)=\{W_\ell:\ell\in s\}\) and, for \(v\in J(s)\), define \(z_v(s)=z_\ell\) for any \(\ell\in s\) satisfying \(W_\ell=v\). Statewise label-value coherence makes \(z_v(s)\) single valued, and \(J(\emptyset)=\emptyset\).

definitions.name: Context-specific response-null certificate
definitions.construction: For a finite binary LS-ADMMG \(\bar G\), let \(\mathcal H_{\mathrm{CS}}(\bar G,A,a,Y,\mathcal U)\) be the set of tuples \(\mathfrak H=(e_\star,\Omega_\star,\lambda_0,h,\mathbf f,\rho)\) with the following properties. The tuple \((\mathbf f,\rho)\) specifies a finite canonical CS-SCM skeleton compatible with \(\bar G\): there is one mutually independent exogenous source for each bidirected edge copy, parent only of that copy's endpoints, one mutually independent finite local source for each observed vertex, deterministic structural maps of the directed parents, and the serious-selector override at every child of \(S\). Every source state space is the graph-determined finite set of deterministic response types of its children. The distinguished source \(U_\star\) belongs to edge copy \(e_\star\), its graph-determined state space is \(\Omega_\star\), and all other source probabilities are collected in \(\rho\). The vector \(\lambda_0\) is a probability mass function on \(\Omega_\star\), \(\sum_{t\in\Omega_\star}h(t)=0\), and \(\lambda_0(t)>|h(t)|\) for every \(t\). Holding \((\mathbf f,\rho)\) fixed, define \(K_{\mathrm{obs}}(x,s\mid t)=\Pr_{\mathbf f,\rho}(X_{V^\circ}=x,S=s\mid U_\star=t)\) and \(K_Q(x_Y\mid t)=\Pr_{\mathbf f,\rho}(X_Y(a,S=\emptyset)=x_Y\mid U_\star=t)\) by summing the finite product law of all other sources. Finally require \(\sum_t h(t)K_{\mathrm{obs}}(x,s\mid t)=0\) for every \((x,s)\), and require \(\sum_t h(t)K_Q(x_Y\mid t)\ne0\) for at least one \(x_Y\).

definitions.name: Two-arm selector-aware parity CS-SCM
definitions.construction: The domain of \(M_\kappa=\operatorname{CSParity}(\bar G,d,y,b_L,b_R,P_L,P_R,e_\star,\pi,\kappa)\) consists of tuples for which \(d\in A\), \(a_d=1\), \(y\in Y\), \(b_L\) and \(b_R\) are distinct vertices outside \(A\cup Y\), \(P_L\) and \(P_R\) are directed simple paths from \(b_L\) and \(b_R\) to \(y\) with \(\operatorname{v}(P_L)\cap\operatorname{v}(P_R)=\{y\}\), \(e_\star=(b_L\leftrightarrow b_R)\in E^{\leftrightarrow}\), \(d\to y\in E^{\to}\), \(T_j=\operatorname{v}(P_j)\setminus\{y\}\) for \(j\in\{L,R\}\), \((T_L\cup T_R)\cap A=\emptyset\), and \(T_L\subseteq J(u)\) or \(T_R\subseteq J(u)\) for every \(u\in\operatorname{supp}(\pi)\). For every \(s\in\mathcal S\), first evaluate the laidback equations \(X_{b_L}=B_L\), \(X_{b_R}=B_R\), copying along \(P_L\) and \(P_R\), \(X_d=1\), \(X_y=X_d(X_{p_L}\oplus X_{p_R})\), and \(X_v=0\) at remaining vertices; then replace the equation at every \(v\in J(s)\) by \(X_v=z_v(s)\). Here \(S\sim\pi\), \(U_\star=(B_L,B_R)\perp S\), \(\Pr_\kappa(B_L=i,B_R=j)=\tfrac14[1+\kappa(-1)^{i+j}]\), and all other exogenous sources are mutually independent and degenerate.

definitions.name: Context-setting interventional target law
definitions.construction: For every compatible CS-SCM \(M\), define \(Q_M(x_Y)=p_M(X_Y(a,S=\emptyset)=x_Y)=p_M(X_Y=x_Y\mid\operatorname{do}(A=a,S=\emptyset))\). For the parity model, write \(Q_\kappa=Q_{M_\kappa}\).

definitions.name: Uncovered SS-ID final failure class
definitions.construction: \(\mathcal C_{\mathrm{cs2}}\) is the class of finite SS-ID inputs for which Algorithm 1 calls Algorithm 2 on a district \(D^\ast\) with \(S\in C=\operatorname{cl}(D^\ast)\) and \(\operatorname{ch}^\ast(S)=\operatorname{ch}(S)\cap(C\setminus D^\ast)\ne\emptyset\), and for which every supported selector state \(s\) that is laidback for \(D^\ast\), serious for a subset of \(\operatorname{ch}^\ast(S)\), and consistent with \(a_{\operatorname{spa}(D^\ast)}\) fails at least one of Algorithm 2 line 7's graphical independence and reachability conditions. Equivalently, the loop exhausts all candidates and reaches the final bare failure return at line 9.

definitions.name: Response-null and support-pooling checks
definitions.construction: First enumerate the finite canonical binary response maps compatible with \(\bar G\), one finite independent exogenous source for every bidirected edge copy, and one finite independent local source for every observed vertex. For each distinguished edge source \(U_\star\), form \(K_{\mathrm{obs}}(x,s\mid t)\) and \(K_Q(x_Y\mid t)\), and solve the explicit semialgebraic feasibility system in \(\lambda_0\), \(h\), and the remaining source probabilities given in the response-null certificate definition. Independently compute \(R=\operatorname{an}_{\bar G^{[\emptyset]}_{V^\circ\setminus A}}(Y)\) and accept a support-pooling identification certificate when, for every \(s\in\operatorname{supp}(\pi)\), \(A\subseteq J(s)\), \((z_v(s))_{v\in A}=a\), and \(J(s)\cap R=\emptyset\), and when \(S\notin\operatorname{an}_{\bar G^{[\emptyset]}_{V\setminus A}}(R)\).

statements.statement: For every two-arm parity-severing configuration in the domain of \(M_\kappa=\operatorname{CSParity}(\bar G,d,y,b_L,b_R,P_L,P_R,e_\star,\pi,\kappa)\) and every \(\kappa\in(-1,1)\), \(M_\kappa\) is a finite binary CS-SCM compatible with \(\bar G\). The selector, the edge-local source \(U_\star=(B_L,B_R)\), and all remaining degenerate exogenous sources are mutually independent, and \(U_\star\) is a parent only of the endpoints of \(e_\star\). The structural equations are defined for every \(s\in\mathcal S\), and \(P_{u,\kappa}=p_{M_\kappa}(X_{V^\circ}\mid S=u)\) is well defined for every \(u\in\mathcal U=\operatorname{supp}(\pi)\).
statements.justification: This lemma verifies that the totalized two-arm construction is a legitimate finite binary CS-SCM.
statements.gap: It does not extract a witness from an arbitrary Algorithm 2 terminal trace.
statements.consumer: It licenses the supporting parity and normalization calculations.

statements.statement:
For every two-arm parity-severing configuration in the domain of \(M_\kappa\), every \(u\in\mathcal U=\operatorname{supp}(\pi)\), and every \(\kappa\in(-1,1)\), \(P_{u,\kappa}=P_{u,0}\). If \(T_L\subseteq J(u)\), then the full response vector is a function of \(B_R\) and selector-fixed constants only, and
\[\Pr_\kappa(B_R=j\mid S=u)=\sum_{i=0}^1\frac14\{1+\kappa(-1)^{i+j}\}=\frac12.\]
The symmetric identity holds if \(T_R\subseteq J(u)\). Since the domain requires one of these two inclusions at every supported selector state, the full observed joint laws agree:
\[p_{M_\kappa}(X_{V^\circ}=x,S=s)=p_{M_0}(X_{V^\circ}=x,S=s)\quad\text{for every }(x,s).\]
Under \(\operatorname{do}(A=a,S=\emptyset)\), the parity-gate coordinate satisfies \(X_y=B_L\oplus B_R\), and therefore
\[Q_\kappa(X_y=0)=\Pr_\kappa(B_L\oplus B_R=0)=\frac{1+\kappa}{2}.\]
Consequently \(M_{1/2}\) and \(M_{-1/2}\) agree on the full observed SS-ID input but disagree on the context-setting target.
statements.justification: The theorem proves exact equality of every supported context law and of the full observed joint law while the context-setting target changes with \(\kappa\).
statements.gap: Its whole-arm premise is not implied by the terminal trace, so it is supporting rather than headline content.
statements.consumer: It supplies the finite normalization proposition and a reusable correlation-hiding example.

statements.statement:
In the smallest two-arm parity-severing configuration with \(P_L=(b_L\to y)\), \(P_R=(b_R\to y)\), \(e_\star=(b_L\leftrightarrow b_R)\), and \(d\to y\), let \(u\in\operatorname{supp}(\pi)\) satisfy \(T_L=\{b_L\}\subseteq J(u)\), \(d\notin J(u)\), and \(b_R\notin J(u)\). Put \(c=z_{b_L}(u)\) and define \(h_u(r)=z_y(u)\) if \(y\in J(u)\), while \(h_u(r)=c\oplus r\) if \(y\notin J(u)\). Then
\[p_{M_\kappa}(X_{b_L}=x_L,X_{b_R}=x_R,X_y=x_y\mid S=u)=\frac12\mathbf 1\{x_L=c\}\mathbf 1\{x_y=h_u(x_R)\},\]
independently of \(\kappa\). Under the context-setting intervention \(\operatorname{do}(d=1,S=\emptyset)\),
\[p_{M_\kappa}(X_y=0\mid\operatorname{do}(d=1,S=\emptyset))=\frac{1+\kappa}{2}.\]
statements.justification: The proposition audits the smallest supplied-context law and the intervention \(\operatorname{do}(S=\emptyset)\) without a selector-mass factor.
statements.gap: It is a special three-coordinate calculation, not an algorithmic completeness result.
statements.consumer: It prevents recurrence of the natural-event normalization error.

statements.statement: For every \(\mathcal I\in\mathcal C_{\mathrm{cs2}}\), does the label-set ADMG at the remaining Algorithm 2 terminal return contain an element of \(\mathcal H_{\mathrm{CS}}(\bar G,A,a,Y,\mathcal U)\)?
statements.justification: The answer is negative in the substantive sense: an identified final-return input cannot carry any sound nonidentification hedge. The formal OEQ replacement is deferred until the mandated target and graph definitions are applied.
statements.gap: The old two-arm class was too narrow, but the stronger obstruction is that terminal failure itself does not imply nonidentification.
statements.consumer: Its replacement theorem will state the explicit four-vertex counterexample after the structured revision.

statements.statement:
On finite binary CS-SCM inputs, \(\mathsf{SS\text{-}ID}\) is sound but not complete. There is a four-vertex LS-ADMMG on \(\{S,A_0,Y_0,W_0\}\), with directed edges \(S\to A_0\to Y_0\) and \(S\to W_0\), an unlabelled edge \(S\leftrightarrow Y_0\), an \(A_0\)-labelled edge copy \(A_0\leftrightarrow Y_0\), and positive selector support on two states that both seriously assign \(A_0=a_0\) and differ only in whether \(W_0\) is seriously assigned, for which the algorithm reaches the final bare failure return of Algorithm 2, while
\[p(X_{Y_0}(a_0,S=\emptyset)=y_0)=p(X_{Y_0}=y_0)\]
for every compatible CS-SCM and every \(y_0\in\{0,1\}\). Thus failure at that return does not imply nonidentification. The published nonidentification conclusions for Algorithm 1 lines 4 and 14 and Algorithm 2 line 3 are unaffected.
statements.justification: The theorem states the corrected frontier: published soundness survives, but one identified line-9 input disproves completeness.
statements.gap: A complete augmented identification algorithm remains to be developed.
statements.consumer: It separates the uncovered final return from the three published nonidentification returns.

sampling_model.design: A CS-SCM supplies \(p(X_{V^\circ}\mid S=u)\) for every \(u\in\mathcal U\).
sampling_model.units: The unit of analysis is a finite binary CS-SCM with an exogenous selector and independent exogenous sources except for the two coordinates contained in the single edge-local source \(U_\star\).
sampling_model.treatment: The target sets \(A=a\) while retaining the natural selector event \(S=\emptyset\).
sampling_model.overlap: Each supplied context and the target selector state have positive selector probability.
sampling_model.nuisance_estimation: Not applicable: the proposal concerns population-level identification.

bibliography.citation: J. J. R. Lee, A. Ghassami, and I. Shpitser. A General Identification Algorithm for Data Fusion Problems under Systematic Selection. UAI, 2024, PMLR 244:2188--2204.
I. Shpitser and J. Pearl. Identification of Joint Interventional Distributions in Recursive Semi-Markovian Causal Models. AAAI, 2006, pp. 1219--1226.
S. Lee, J. D. Correa, and E. Bareinboim. General Identifiability with Arbitrary Surrogate Experiments. UAI, 2020, PMLR 115:389--398.
Y. Kivva, E. Mokhtarian, J. Etesami, and N. Kiyavash. Revisiting the General Identifiability Problem. UAI, 2022, PMLR 180:1022--1030.

tldr: The completeness program is reversed. The exact \(\mathsf{SS\text{-}ID}\) target is the context-setting intervention \(p(X_Y(a,S=\emptyset))\), and the input is the full observed joint law \(p(X_{V^\circ},S)\). A support-pooling family reaches the final bare return of Algorithm 2 even though every positive selector stratum implements \(A=a\), so the target is identified by the observed marginal \(p(X_Y)\). Thus the final return is not a universal nonidentification certificate: the published algorithm remains sound but is not complete. The two-arm parity calculation is retained only as a supporting cancellation and normalization check.

project_justification.gap: Algorithm 2 searches for one supported selector context that simultaneously passes a context-specific independence test and a fixing-reachability test. It does not pool selector strata that all implement the same intervention. Consequently its final return can occur at an identified query.
project_justification.niche: The graph-native contribution is a support-pooling identification certificate at the exact uncovered Algorithm 2 line-9 trace, not a new nonidentification hedge. Its smallest member has an unlabelled \(S\leftrightarrow Y\) edge causing every eligible one-context independence test to fail, while all supported contexts set the treatment cutset to the same target value.
project_justification.fill: If all positive selector states seriously assign \(A=a\), do not seriously assign target ancestors downstream of \(A\), and \(A\) cuts every directed path from \(S\) to those ancestors, then \(X_Y(a,S=\emptyset)=X_Y\) almost surely and the identifying functional is \(p(X_Y)\). A four-vertex LS-ADMMG satisfies these conditions and reaches the final Algorithm 2 failure, refuting the completeness conjecture and motivating a support-pooling precheck.

related_work: The soundness theorem of \cite{LeeGhassamiShpitser2024Systematic} remains intact, as does their nonidentification theorem for Algorithm 1 lines 4 and 14 and Algorithm 2 line 3. Their final Algorithm 2 return is outside that theorem, and the support-pooling family shows that this omission cannot be filled by assigning a nonidentification certificate to every final return. Ordinary Shpitser--Pearl hedges preserve one observational law and certify nonidentification; they cannot represent an identified algorithmic failure. Hedgelets and thickets of \cite{LeeCorreaBareinboim2020General} preserve collections of surrogate experimental laws, whereas the present obstruction is that selector strata implement the target experiment only after pooling their coherent joint law. The selector-aware parity cancellation is not claimed as the headline because the supplementary construction of \cite{LeeGhassamiShpitser2024Systematic} already uses the same serious-assignment uniformization mechanism in greater generality.
interpretation: When every observed selector state forces \(A=a\), the observed population has already undergone the target treatment intervention, even if the selector is arbitrarily confounded with the outcome. If fixing \(A\) removes every directed route from \(S\) to \(Y\), setting \(S=\emptyset\) changes no outcome mechanism. Marginalizing the observed joint law over \(S\) therefore gives the target directly. Algorithm 2 misses this because it demands a valid single-context bridge rather than recognizing the support-wide experiment.
technical_internal_limitation: The result refutes completeness but does not characterize all identifiable final-return inputs or furnish a complete replacement algorithm. The support-pooling certificate is sufficient, finite, and graph readable. The explicit counterexample is binary and restricts support to contexts assigning one treatment value; mixtures of treatment values require additional transport or fixing arguments.
honest_scope: No universal CS-hedge or terminal nonidentification theorem is claimed. Positive mass at the laidback target context is neither assumed nor needed. The parity model proves only a restricted conditional-law cancellation and a finite normalization check. The substantive conclusion is negative for the published conjecture: on the displayed finite binary input, \(\mathsf{SS\text{-}ID}\) reaches its uncovered final return although the causal target has the explicit functional \(p(X_Y)\).
